allergens: gluten (possible soy sauce in dipping sauce)

serving.description: 1 set for 2 persons (approx. 700 g total edible portion shared)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 350 g of the shared set, representing half of a whole steamed seabass (around 200 g fish flesh) plus jasmine rice and accompaniments, as typically served at a Thai restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed sea bass, flesh — FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked sea bass (NDB 15060) and HPB Singapore nutrient data for steamed fish dishes
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS; USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for a Thai steamed seabass set meal as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA steamed fish data and USDA SR Legacy cooked sea bass (NDB 15060), blended with HPB Singapore steamed fish dish data to reflect the mixed set-meal composition (fish flesh ~57%, jasmine rice ~30%, sauce and vegetables ~13% by weight). Values represent the composite per-100 g edible portion of the full set.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — steamed white rice (jasmine) GI ~54–56; fish and sauce components contribute negligible glycemic load
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a Thai steamed seabass set meal. The GI of 54 is derived from the dominant glycemic contributor in the set: steamed jasmine rice, which has a GI of approximately 54–56 per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. This places the composite GI in the low category. The fish, lime sauce, and vegetable components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrate. The composite GI of 54 is a weighted estimate reflecting the rice fraction (~30% by weight, ~80% of total carbohydrate). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 11.4 × 3.5 × 0.30) / 100 ≈ 6.2 g per 350 g serving portion.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and dipping sauce
hypertension.guidance: The set meal contains approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g, primarily from fish sauce and lime-chilli dipping sauce; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and limit dipping sauce use to keep a 350 g serving within 1,470 mg sodium, which approaches the 1,500 mg daily target for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by jasmine rice; high lean protein from seabass
type2_diabetes.guidance: The steamed seabass component is low-GI and high in lean protein, which supports glycaemic control; the jasmine rice contributes to a composite GI of approximately 54 (low), so individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should still be mindful of rice portion size, but this dish is generally favourable due to its overall low GI and high protein content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish protein, low saturated fat, omega-3 fatty acids from seabass
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed seabass is a lean, low-saturated-fat protein source providing omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are associated with reduced cardiovascular risk; the steaming preparation avoids added fats, making this set meal a heart-favourable choice when fish sauce is used in moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Steamed Seabass Set for Two (ชุดปลากะพงนึ่งสำหรับ 2 คน) is a Thai restaurant set meal centred on a whole or large-portion Asian seabass (Lates calcarifer, known in Thai as pla kapong) that is steamed whole and served with a classic Thai lime-chilli-fish sauce dressing (nam jim seafood), steamed jasmine rice, and typically a clear soup or stir-fried vegetables. The dish is a staple of Thai seafood cuisine and is widely served in Thai restaurants across Singapore, including at establishments such as Sanook Kitchen. The seabass is prized for its firm, moist, white flesh and mild, slightly sweet flavour that absorbs the bright, sour, and spicy notes of the lime-garlic-chilli sauce. Steaming preserves the natural moisture and nutrients of the fish without added fat, resulting in a lean, high-protein dish with low saturated fat content. Nutritionally, the set provides approximately 118 kcal per 100 g of the composite meal, with 12.8 g protein, 2.1 g fat, and 11.4 g carbohydrate, the carbohydrate coming predominantly from the jasmine rice component. The dish is a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and omega-3 fatty acids. The primary nutritional concern is sodium from fish sauce and dipping sauce, which can be significant for individuals managing hypertension. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the low glycemic index of approximately 54 (driven by jasmine rice) means portion control of the rice is still advisable, but the dish is generally favourable. The lean fish protein and omega-3 content make this set meal a cardiovascular-friendly choice. The set is halal-suitable and dairy-free. It is commonly ordered as a shared meal for two diners in a Thai restaurant context.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for a Thai steamed seabass set meal as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA steamed fish data, USDA SR Legacy cooked sea bass (NDB 15060), and HPB Singapore steamed fish dish data, blended to reflect the composite set-meal composition. GI is estimated from the dominant glycemic contributor (jasmine rice) using the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium because the values are defensible adaptations from approved sources but are not an exact match to this specific restaurant dish.
notes: 1. The food_name in the input ('6919D $43.80 Steam Seabass Set for 2 $43') contains menu-code and price clutter; the canonical English name has been normalised to 'Thai Steamed Seabass Set for Two'. 2. The input cuisine tags include 'Curry' and 'Pad Thai', which are not relevant to this dish and have been disregarded. 3. Nutrient values represent the composite per-100 g edible portion of the full set meal (fish flesh ~57%, jasmine rice ~30%, sauce and vegetables ~13% by weight), adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA SR Legacy NDB 15060, and HPB Singapore data. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; this is confirmed zero as steamed fish and jasmine rice contain no industrially produced trans fats (naturally zero by preparation method and ingredient composition). 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.7), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6), potassium_mg (280.0), calcium_mg (28.0), iron_mg (0.7), cholesterol_mg (42.0), vitamin_c_mg (4.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (18.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.08), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.07), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (2.4), vitamin_b6_mg (0.18), vitamin_b12_mcg (1.1), folate_mcg_dfe (12.0), vitamin_d_mcg (4.2), vitamin_e_mg (0.6), phosphorus_mg (185.0), magnesium_mg (26.0), zinc_mg (0.6), selenium_mcg (18.0), water_g (72.0) are all estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy NDB 15060 for cooked sea bass, adjusted for the composite set-meal composition. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record as all fields have been estimated from approved sources. 6. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on the jasmine rice component per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care), categorised as low GI. 7. FoodOn FOODON_03302051 and FoodEx2 A0CKP are used as closest_parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific Thai set meal. 8. Halal status is assessed as likely true for a Thai restaurant context in Singapore, but individual restaurant certification should be verified.
